title: How Much Does It Cost to Start a Dog Walking Business?
content:
Starting a Dog Walking Business typically costs between $200 and $2,000, depending on your location, scale, and approach. Insurance, a Rover/Wag profile, and comfortable shoes. The lowest startup cost of any pet business. Individual walks at $15-$25 each, but walking 3-4 dogs simultaneously generates $45-$100/hour.

Quick Cost Summary

Costs are estimates based on national averages.

Detailed Cost Breakdown

Equipment & Supplies - $60 to $600

The core tools and supplies for your dog walking business. Start with essentials, upgrade as revenue justifies. Quality matters for client-facing work - save everywhere else.

Business Formation & Insurance - $200 to $1,500

LLC formation ($50-$250), general liability insurance ($300-$1,000/year), and any industry-specific coverage. One claim without insurance can end your business.

Marketing & Client Acquisition - $100 to $1,500

Google Business Profile (free, essential), a simple website ($12-20/month), and initial outreach. Word-of-mouth and reviews drive most dog walking business businesses after the first 10 clients.

Software & Technology - $0 to $500

Scheduling, invoicing, and payment tools. Start free (Google Calendar, Wave) and upgrade when volume demands it.

What Most People Forget

Hidden costs that catch first-time dog walking business owners off guard.

Self-Employment Taxes (15.3% of net earnings)

15.3% of net earnings for Social Security and Medicare on top of income tax. Set aside 25-30% of every dollar.

Seasonal Revenue Variation (20-50% seasonal decline)

Most dog walking business businesses see 20-50% revenue swings between peak and slow periods. Save during peaks to cover fixed costs during dips.

Equipment Maintenance & Replacement ($1,000-$5,000/year)

Commercial equipment needs regular maintenance and eventual replacement. Budget accordingly from month one.

Insurance Premium Increases (5-15% annual increase)

Premiums rise 5-15% annually. Any claim triggers higher renewals. Shop 3-5 insurers every year.

The Small Costs That Add Up ($3,000-$10,000/year)

Software subscriptions, fuel, supplies, repairs - individually trivial, collectively $3,000-$10,000/year.

How Long Does It Take?

Plan for 1 to 2 weeks.

Business Setup (1-2 weeks): Form LLC, get insurance, obtain required licenses.

Equipment & Preparation (1-4 weeks): Acquire equipment, set up workspace or vehicle.

Marketing & First Clients (1-4 weeks): Launch online presence and secure first paying clients.

Growth (Months 2-2): Build to consistent revenue through referrals and marketing.

How Long Until You're Profitable?

Most dog walking business owners reach profitability within 1 to 2 months.

A dog walking business with $200-$2,000 in startup costs typically reaches monthly breakeven within 1-2 months. Track your monthly breakeven number from day one.

How to Start for Less

Start Small and Scale with Revenue (Save 20-40% of startup costs)

Launch the minimum viable version of your dog walking business and upgrade only as revenue justifies it.

Buy Used Equipment (Save $2,000-$20,000)

Commercial equipment from closing businesses sells at 30-60% of retail.

Leverage Free Marketing First (Save $500-$3,000)

Google Business Profile, Nextdoor, Facebook groups, and word-of-mouth before paid ads.

Negotiate Everything (Save 5-15% on major expenses)

Lease terms, vendor pricing, insurance rates - all negotiable.

Track Every Dollar from Day One (Save Prevents $2,000-$10,000 in penalties)

Use QuickBooks or Wave from your first transaction. Clean books prevent tax surprises.

Tools & Resources

Accounting: QuickBooks - Track income, expenses, and taxes for your dog walking business.

Business Insurance: Next Insurance - General liability and professional coverage for dog walking business businesses.

Business Formation: LegalZoom - Form your LLC. Liability protection is essential.

Payments: Square - Accept card payments, send invoices. Free reader, no monthly fees.

Website: Squarespace - Professional site with services, pricing, and contact info for your dog walking business.

Payroll: Gusto - Payroll, tax withholding, and benefits when you hire employees.

Some links are affiliate links. We may earn a commission at no extra cost to you.

Comparing Startup Costs

  • Pet Sitting Business - Natural companion service. Most dog walkers also offer pet sitting and vice versa.
  • Dog Training Business - Higher per-session revenue ($75-$150/hr) but requires certification and specialized skills.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to start a dog walking business?

Startup costs range from $200 to $2,000 depending on scale, location, and equipment choices. The low end is a bootstrapped solo operation; the high end is a fully equipped professional launch.

How much do dog walking business owners make?

Income varies by pricing, volume, and model. Solo operators typically earn $40,000-$100,000/year. Owners who hire and scale can earn $80,000-$200,000+.

Is a dog walking business profitable?

Yes - well-run dog walking business businesses generate 10-25% net margins once established. Profitability depends on pricing, cost control, and consistent volume.

Do I need a license for a dog walking business?

At minimum, a general business license ($50-$200). Some dog walking business businesses require industry-specific licenses or certifications. Check your state and local requirements.

How do I get customers for a dog walking business?

Google Business Profile, Nextdoor, local Facebook groups, and asking every satisfied customer for referrals. Most dog walking business businesses build a sustainable client base within 2-4 months.

How long does it take to start a dog walking business?

Plan for 1-2 weeks from decision to first revenue. Timeline depends on licensing, equipment acquisition, and client building.

About the Author

Michael Oxton spent $180,000 starting Night Shift Brewing in 2012 - and got a meaningful chunk of that wrong. Over 13 years, he grew it into one of New England's largest independent breweries before selling the company. Every cost breakdown on this site is informed by the expensive lessons of actually doing it.
